request.question: Justin Hurwitz is best known for scoring an American musical romantic comedy-drama film that stars who as a jazz pianist?

title: Gil Evans
Blue Hawaii
La La Land (film)
Justin Hurwitz
Gigi (Hank Jones album)
Iiro Rantala
From Justin to Kelly
City of Stars
List of accolades received by La La Land
High School Musical (franchise)
sentences: Ian Ernest Gilmore "Gil" Evans (born Green; May 13, 1912 – March 20, 1988) was a Canadian jazz pianist, arranger, composer and bandleader.
He is widely recognized as one of the greatest orchestrators in jazz, playing an important role in the development of cool jazz, modal jazz, free jazz and jazz fusion.
He is best known for his acclaimed collaborations with Miles Davis.
Blue Hawaii is a 1961 American musical romantic comedy film set in the state of Hawaii and starring Elvis Presley.
The screenplay by Hal Kanter was nominated by the Writers Guild of America in 1962 in the category of Best Written American Musical.

The movie opened at no. 2 in box office receipts for that week and despite mixed reviews from critics, finished as the 10th top-grossing movie of 1961 and 14th for 1962 on the "Variety" national box office survey, earning $5 million.
The film won a fourth place prize Laurel Award in the category of Top Musical of 1961.
La La Land is a 2016 American musical romantic comedy-drama film written and directed by Damien Chazelle.
It stars Ryan Gosling as a jazz pianist and Emma Stone as an aspiring actress, who meet and fall in love in Los Angeles while pursuing their dreams.
The film's title refers simultaneously to music, the city of Los Angeles, and to the idiom for being out of touch with reality.
Justin Hurwitz (born 22 January 1985) is an American film composer and a television writer.
He is best known for scoring the film "La La Land".
He is a frequent collaborator with director Damien Chazelle and also scored Chazelle's two other features, "Guy and Madeline on a Park Bench" and "Whiplash".

Gigi (full title Hank Jones Swings Songs from Lerner and Loewe's Gigi ) is an album by American jazz pianist Hank Jones featuring jazz adaptations of tunes from Alan Jay Lerner and Frederick Loewe's musical romantic comedy film "Gigi" recorded in 1958 and released on the Golden Crest label.
Iiro Rantala (born 1970, in Helsinki) is a Finnish jazz pianist.
He studied piano in the jazz department of Sibelius Academy and classical piano at the Manhattan School of Music.
He is one of the best known Finnish jazz pianists, both in Finland and abroad.
Rantala is a pianist and composer with Trio Töykeät, a Finnish jazz trio.
In addition to jazz, he has composed some classical pieces, most notably the "Concerto for Piano and Concerto in G♯ΔA♭".
From Justin to Kelly is a 2003 American musical romantic comedy film written by Kim Fuller and directed by Robert Iscove.
The film, starring "American Idol" first season winner Kelly Clarkson and runner-up Justin Guarini, won the Golden Raspberry Award for Worst 'Musical' of Our First 25 Years in 2005.
This film is often regarded as one of the worst movies ever made.
"City of Stars" is a song performed by Ryan Gosling and Emma Stone from the film "La La Land" (2016).
The music of the song was composed by Justin Hurwitz while the lyrics were provided by Benj Pasek and Justin Paul.
The song won numerous awards, including the award for Best Original Song at both the 74th Golden Globe Awards and 89th Academy Awards.
"La La Land" is a 2016 American romantic musical comedy-drama film written and directed by Damien Chazelle.
Starring Ryan Gosling and Emma Stone, the film focuses on two young people struggling to make ends meet in Los Angeles, while pursuing their dreams as artists.
Justin Hurwitz composed the film's musical score, while Linus Sandgren was the cinematographer.
David and Sandy Reynolds-Wasco were responsible for the production design and Mary Zophres designed the costumes.
The "High School Musical" franchise consists of four Disney musical romantic comedy-drama films directed by Kenny Ortega and created by Peter Barsocchini.
It stars Zac Efron, Vanessa Hudgens, Ashley Tisdale, Lucas Grabeel, Corbin Bleu, and Monique Coleman.
The original film was released simply as a Disney Channel Original Movie, but after its huge success, it was followed by a television sequel.
The received a theatrical release.
Tisdale and Grabeel reprised their roles as Sharpay and Ryan Evans in a spin-off on the series titled "Sharpay's Fabulous Adventure", it was a direct to video film.
Numerous international versions have been created, plus stage musicals and video games have been released, among other things.
On March 1, 2016, a fourth installment was announced.
The films have developed a cult following.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Justin Hurwitz is best known for scoring an American musical romantic comedy-drama film that stars who as a jazz pianist?
hop1_docs: List of accolades received by La La Land | "La La Land" is a 2016 American romantic musical comedy-drama film written and directed by Damien Chazelle.  Starring Ryan Gosling and Emma Stone, the film focuses on two young people struggling to make ends meet in Los Angeles, while pursuing their dreams as artists.  Justin Hurwitz composed the film's musical score, while Linus Sandgren was the cinematographer.  David and Sandy Reynolds-Wasco were responsible for the production design and Mary Zophres designed the costumes.

Justin Hurwitz | Justin Hurwitz (born 22 January 1985) is an American film composer and a television writer.  He is best known for scoring the film "La La Land".  He is a frequent collaborator with director Damien Chazelle and also scored Chazelle's two other features, "Guy and Madeline on a Park Bench" and "Whiplash".

La La Land (film) | La La Land is a 2016 American musical romantic comedy-drama film written and directed by Damien Chazelle.  It stars Ryan Gosling as a jazz pianist and Emma Stone as an aspiring actress, who meet and fall in love in Los Angeles while pursuing their dreams.  The film's title refers simultaneously to music, the city of Los Angeles, and to the idiom for being out of touch with reality.

The Fabulous Baker Boys | The Fabulous Baker Boys is a 1989 American romantic comedy-drama musical film written and directed by Steve Kloves, and starring real life brothers Jeff Bridges and Beau Bridges as two brothers struggling to make a living as lounge jazz pianists in Seattle.  In desperation, they take on a female singer, Michelle Pfeiffer, who revitalizes their careers, causing the brothers to re-examine their relationship with each other and with their music.
Upstage (film) | Upstage (also known as The Mask of Comedy) is a 1926 American silent romantic drama film directed by Monta Bell, starring Norma Shearer and New York musical comedy star Oscar Shaw.

From Justin to Kelly | From Justin to Kelly is a 2003 American musical romantic comedy film written by Kim Fuller and directed by Robert Iscove.  The film, starring "American Idol" first season winner Kelly Clarkson and runner-up Justin Guarini, won the Golden Raspberry Award for Worst 'Musical' of Our First 25 Years in 2005.  This film is often regarded as one of the worst movies ever made.

List of Amy Adams performances | Amy Adams is an American actress who made her film debut in the 1999 black comedy "Drop Dead Gorgeous".  She went on to guest star in a variety of television shows, including "That '70s Show", "Charmed", "Buffy the Vampire Slayer", and "The Office", and also appeared in minor film roles.  In 2002, she had her first major role in Steven Spielberg's biographical crime drama "Catch Me If You Can".  However, the film did not launch her career as Spielberg had hoped.  Three years later, she made the breakthrough with the comedy-drama "Junebug" (2005), for which she received her first Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress nomination.  Adams also appeared in the romantic comedy "The Wedding Date" that same year.  In 2007, she starred in the Disney romantic comedy "Enchanted", for which she won the Saturn Award for Best Actress, and was nominated for her first Golden Globe Award for Best Actress (Comedy or Musical).

response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Justin Hurwitz — [2]: "best known for scoring the film "La La Land"."
- "Justin Hurwitz composed the film's musical score" — [1]
- "La La Land" is a 2016 American romantic musical comedy-drama film — [1]: ""La La Land" is a 2016 American romantic musical comedy-drama film"
- Ryan Gosling — [3]: "It stars Ryan Gosling as a jazz pianist"
- Emma Stone — [3]: "Emma Stone as an aspiring actress"

Ambiguities:
- None — the passages consistently link "Justin Hurwitz" to "La La Land" and state "Ryan Gosling as a jazz pianist."

Bridge / missing fact:
- None — the passage [3] explicitly gives the needed relation: who stars as the jazz pianist.

Retrieval direction:
- "La La Land (film)"
- "Justin Hurwitz"
- "Ryan Gosling"
Final conclusion: Ryan Gosling.

hop2_docs: La La Land (film) | La La Land is a 2016 American musical romantic comedy-drama film written and directed by Damien Chazelle.  It stars Ryan Gosling as a jazz pianist and Emma Stone as an aspiring actress, who meet and fall in love in Los Angeles while pursuing their dreams.  The film's title refers simultaneously to music, the city of Los Angeles, and to the idiom for being out of touch with reality.

List of accolades received by La La Land | "La La Land" is a 2016 American romantic musical comedy-drama film written and directed by Damien Chazelle.  Starring Ryan Gosling and Emma Stone, the film focuses on two young people struggling to make ends meet in Los Angeles, while pursuing their dreams as artists.  Justin Hurwitz composed the film's musical score, while Linus Sandgren was the cinematographer.  David and Sandy Reynolds-Wasco were responsible for the production design and Mary Zophres designed the costumes.

City of Stars | "City of Stars" is a song performed by Ryan Gosling and Emma Stone from the film "La La Land" (2016).  The music of the song was composed by Justin Hurwitz while the lyrics were provided by Benj Pasek and Justin Paul.  The song won numerous awards, including the award for Best Original Song at both the 74th Golden Globe Awards and 89th Academy Awards.

Olivia Courtney | Olivia Chauntelle Fowler-Courtney (born March 26, 1992) is an American artistic gymnast, former US National Team member, and 11 time All American.  She placed 10th All Around at the 2008 Olympic trials in Philadelphia, Pa.  Olivia is a professional stunt performer, and the traffic gymnast in the seven time 2017 Golden Globes and six time 2017 Oscars winning film La La Land.  Directed by Damien Chazelle starring Emma Stone, Ryan Gosling featuring John Legend.

Jazz à la Bohemia | Jazz à la Bohemia (also released as Greenwich Village Jazz) is a live album by American jazz pianist Randy Weston's trio with saxophonist Cecil Payne which was recorded in 1956 at the Café Bohemia in Greenwich Village and released on the Riverside label.

La Vie de Bohème (album) | La Vie de Bohème is a studio album released by jazz pianist Dave Burrell.  The album is Burrell's take on the operatic adaptation of La Vie de Bohème by Giacomo Puccini, titled "La bohème".  The album has been called "a fine example of the similarities between the free jazz and classical worlds."  Though this is not a straight performance of the opera, each of the acts are represented with "a great deal of improvisation."

La Leyenda de La Hora | La Leyenda de La Hora (The Legend of the Hour) is a 1981 album by jazz pianist McCoy Tyner released on the Columbia label.  It features performances by Tyner with Paquito D'Rivera, Chico Freeman, Marcus Belgrave, Hubert Laws, Bobby Hutcherson and a string section.
